explanation: The requirement states that the self-test procedure (a system checking process) should confirm that the expiratory valve is functional. The atomic propositions available are 'selfTestPass' (all self-test steps passed successfully), 'testPSExpPass' (test for expiratory valve passes), and 'testPSExpFail' (test for expiratory valve fails), as well as 'testPSExpSkip' (skipping test). To confirm the expiratory valve is functional as part of the self-test, we want to ensure: whenever self-test as a whole passes, the subtest for the expiratory valve must also have passed (i.e., if selfTestPass is true, then testPSExpPass is true as well). This is best captured by 'G (bool_exp1 -> bool_exp3)' (template 30) where bool_exp1 is 'selfTestPass' and bool_exp3 is 'testPSExpPass'. This ensures that in all paths, if the self-test passes, the confirmation about the expiratory valve's functionality test must also be true.

The natural language requires that the self-test procedure must verify the expiratory valve is functional. From the available atomic propositions, 'selfTestPass' indicates all self-test steps passed successfully, while 'testPSExpPass' and 'testPSExpFail' indicate whether the expiratory valve test specifically passes or fails. To capture the requirement, we encode that whenever the self-test is passed (i.e., all self-test steps, presumably including the expiratory valve, succeed), this must only be true when the expiratory valve test is also passed—i.e., it is not possible for selfTestPass to be true if the expiratory valve test failed.

To capture this, we use the template for: 'Globally, if self-test passes, then the expiratory valve test must also pass.' This is expressed as 'G (selfTestPass -> testPSExpPass)'. This ensures that the overall system will only allow the self-test to pass if the expiratory valve test was also a success, which aligns with the confirmation requirement of the natural language specification.

The natural language requirement states that the self-test procedure must confirm the expiratory valve is functional. Within the provided atomic propositions, 'selfTestPass' indicates all self-test steps passed, and 'testPSExpPass' indicates the test for the expiratory valve was successful. To capture the requirement, we use the template 'G (bool_exp1 -> bool_exp3)', which asserts globally: if the self-test passes, then the expiratory valve functionality test must also pass. This captures the requirement that confirmation of the expiratory valve being functional is an integral requirement of the self-test's overall success.

The natural language requirement states that the self-test procedure must confirm that the expiratory valve is functional. In the provided atomic propositions, 'selfTestPass' indicates that all self-test steps passed, and 'testPSExpPass' indicates the expiratory valve test passes (i.e., the expiratory valve is functional). Thus, the LTL property should state: whenever the overall self-test is considered passed, the expiratory valve test must have also passed. This is a safety property that must hold globally over operation: 'G (selfTestPass -> testPSExpPass)'. This ensures that the self-test result is only positive if the crucial sub-test for the expiratory valve passes as well.

The requirement is that the self-test procedure must confirm the expiratory valve is functional. This can be represented by saying that whenever the overall self-test passes (selfTestPass), this must imply that the expiratory valve test passes (testPSExpPass). If the self-test passes but the expiratory valve check does not pass, the requirement would be violated. Thus, we express: G (selfTestPass -> testPSExpPass).

The requirement states that during the self-test procedure, it must be confirmed that the expiratory valve is functional. In the provided atomic propositions, 'selfTestPass' indicates all self-test steps were successful, and 'testPSExpPass' specifically indicates the expiratory valve test passed. The logical formalization is that if the self-test as a whole passes ('selfTestPass'), then necessarily the test for the expiratory valve must have passed as well ('testPSExpPass'). This can be captured with the LTL template: G (bool_exp1 -> bool_exp3), where bool_exp1=selfTestPass and bool_exp3=testPSExpPass. This expresses globally (always), if the overall self-test passes, then the expiratory valve's function test passed, which confirms its functionality was checked and succeeded.

The requirement states that the self-test procedure should confirm the expiratory valve is functional. There are atomic propositions indicating (1) the self-test has passed (selfTestPass), and (2) that the expiratory valve test has passed (testPSExpPass) or failed (testPSExpFail), as well as an explicit proposition for skipping the expiratory valve test (testPSExpSkip).

To reflect the requirement that the self-test should confirm the expiratory valve is functional, the correct logical property is: whenever the self-test passes, the expiratory valve test must also pass. This is naturally modelled as an implication: G(selfTestPass -> testPSExpPass).

G ("globally") means this property must always be true: whenever a self-test pass occurs, it must be the case that the expiratory valve test has been successful at the same instant (or, in a more general sense, the success of the self-test cannot claim the expiratory valve is confirmed unless its test also passes at that point).

This approach captures the input natural language by demanding that passing the self-test implies confirmation that the expiratory valve has been checked and works (is functional).

In the provided atomic propositions, 'selfTestPass' indicates all self-test steps passed successfully, and 'testPSExpPass' indicates the expiratory valve test has succeeded. The logical property needed is that, whenever the self test passes, the expiratory valve test must have passed (since the self-test must confirm proper function). This maps directly to an implication that whenever 'selfTestPass' occurs, 'testPSExpPass' must be true. Thus, we select the LTL template: G (bool_exp1 -> bool_exp3). We set bool_exp1 = selfTestPass and bool_exp3 = testPSExpPass. N_DURATION is not used in this template.

The NL statement requires the self-test to confirm that the expiratory valve is functional. Looking at atomic propositions, 'testPSExpPass' indicates the expiratory valve test passes (success), 'testPSExpFail' indicates the test fails, and 'selfTestPass' indicates all self-test steps passed successfully. To express that the self-test confirms the expiratory valve is functional, we can say: whenever the overall self-test is a pass, the expiratory valve test must also have passed. This is a logical implication (if selfTestPass, then testPSExpPass), and must hold at all times—so we apply 'G (selfTestPass -> testPSExpPass)'. Template 30 is 'G (bool_exp1 -> bool_exp3)', which fits. Here, 'bool_exp1' is 'selfTestPass', 'bool_exp3' is 'testPSExpPass'. N_DURATION is not used in this template.

The requirement is that the self-test procedure should confirm the expiratory valve is functional. The best available atomic propositions are 'testPSExpPass' (indicates the test for functionality of expiratory valve passes), 'testPSExpFail' (indicates the test fails), and 'selfTestPass'. To capture the meaning of confirmation of proper expiratory valve function as part of the self-test, we can specify: whenever the aggregate self-test procedure passes (selfTestPass), it implies the expiratory valve test must have passed (testPSExpPass). This is best represented by the LTL formula G(selfTestPass -> testPSExpPass), meaning globally, whenever self-test succeeded, expiratory valve was confirmed to be functional at that time (its test passed). This is the most direct, correct contextual capture given the available propositions.
